1: David comes to Nob and Ahimelech the priest meets him trembling, asking why he comes alone. The anointed king arriving at the sanctuary in flight — without his usual attendants, in conditions that suggest crisis — is the pattern of Christ's arrival at the Father's house in the temple (John 2:13-17: he arrived and acted without the usual religious escort). Both arrivals at the sanctuary surprise the official custodians. Ahimelech's trembling at an irregular visit echoes the trembling of the religious authorities at Jesus's provocative presence in the temple.
2: David's deceptive cover story — sent by the king on a confidential mission, cannot reveal the details — is a lie that enables the anointed's survival. The moral complexity here is real: the NT never directly addresses this episode, though Jesus uses it in a way that validates the action's outcome. The hiddenness of Christ's true identity from the authorities who would destroy him (he could not walk openly in Judea because the Jews were seeking to kill him, John 7:1) involves a strategic withholding of his full identity in certain contexts — not deception, but discretion about the fullness of revelation until the appointed time.
3: David asks for five loaves or whatever is available. The urgency of the need — no food, no weapons, no attendants — has brought the anointed to depend entirely on what the sanctuary can provide. Christ's dependence on others' provision during his ministry (Luke 8:3: women who supported him out of their means; John 4:8: disciples gone to buy food) is the incarnation's structural dependence — the one who owns everything receives provision from those he has given everything. The anointed in need at the sanctuary is the pattern of the incarnate Son's genuine dependence.
4: Ahimelech offers the showbread — the bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm), the twelve loaves consecrated before YHWH and replaced each Sabbath — with the condition that the men have kept themselves from women. Jesus cites this exact episode when the Pharisees challenge the disciples for plucking grain on the Sabbath: “Have you not read what David did when he was hungry, and those who were with him — how he entered the house of God and ate the bread of the Presence, which it was not lawful for him to eat?” (Matthew 12:3-4; Mark 2:25-26; Luke 6:3-4). Jesus identifies himself with David, his disciples with David's men, and the Sabbath controversy with the showbread controversy: the anointed's needs take priority over ritual law.
5: David's assurance that his men have kept themselves ritually pure invokes the military purity laws. The sacred bread given to men who are genuinely dedicated to the holy cause — even if the specific ritual form is irregular — is the principle Jesus extracts from this episode: “the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. So the Son of Man is lord even of the Sabbath” (Mark 2:27-28). The anointed is not bound by ritual regulations that were made to serve him; he is their lord and their purpose. David eating the showbread and Jesus eating on the Sabbath are both demonstrations of the anointed's authority over the religious law.
6: The priest gives David the sacred bread — the bread that had been before YHWH, replaced that day with fresh loaves. Sacred bread given from the presence of YHWH to the hungry anointed and his men is a type of the eucharist: the bread from the sanctuary, the bread of the divine Presence, distributed by the priest to those who have been consecrated for the anointed's service. The feeding of the anointed and his community with bread from the Lord's table is the pattern that the Lord's Supper enacts: “the bread that we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ?” (1 Corinthians 10:16).
7: Doeg the Edomite — Saul's chief herdsman, detained before YHWH — was present. This detail is inserted because Doeg will later betray this encounter to Saul, triggering the massacre of Nob's priests (1 Samuel 22). The presence of the traitor at the moment of the anointed's vulnerability is a pattern: Judas is at the Last Supper when Jesus distributes the bread (John 13:26-27); a man is in the crowd who will betray the showbread encounter. The traitor is present at the sacred meal, and his presence will lead to the destruction of those who served the anointed.
8: David asks if there is a spear or sword available — he came without weapons because “the king's errand was urgent.” The anointed king without weapons, dependent on what the sanctuary provides, will receive the sword of the enemy he already defeated. Christ comes to earth without the sword (Matthew 26:52: “put your sword back in its place”) — the kingdom he establishes does not advance by the sword but by the word and the cross. The anointed's dependence on provision from beyond himself rather than self-sufficient armed power is consistent from David's dependence at Nob to Christ's crucifixion without self-defense.
9: The priest: “The sword of Goliath the Philistine — the one you struck down in the Valley of Elah — it is wrapped in a cloth behind the ephod. Take it if you want.” David's own earlier victory is preserved at the sanctuary and returned to him in his crisis. The decisive victory that had seemed to belong to the past is still available — it has been kept for this moment. Christ's resurrection is the definitive victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:55-57) kept in reserve and applied in every subsequent crisis of his people: “where, O death, is your victory? where, O death, is your sting?” The sword of the old victory equips the anointed for the new crisis.
10: David flees from Saul and goes to Achish king of Gath — the Philistine king, ruler of Goliath's city. The anointed seeking refuge among the enemies of Israel — the very enemies he has spent his military career defeating — is the extreme of the exile pattern. Christ's exile in Egypt as an infant (Matthew 2:14-15) fulfills Hosea 11:1: “Out of Egypt I called my son” — the Messiah's survival required refuge among Egypt, another historical enemy of Israel. The anointed in the land of his enemies, preserved by YHWH's hand even there, is the pattern of divine sovereignty over hostile territory.
11: Achish's servants identify David: “Isn't this David, king of the land? Isn't this the one they sing about — ‘Saul has struck down his thousands, and David his ten thousands’?” The Philistines identify David as the king of the land before his own people have fully acknowledged it; the foreign servants apply the royal title that Saul refuses to acknowledge. Pilate's placard “King of the Jews” (John 19:19) and the Roman soldiers' “Hail, King of the Jews!” (Matthew 27:29) are the same ironic pattern: foreigners acknowledge the anointed's true identity while the covenant people's leaders deny it.
12: David was very afraid of Achish king of Gath. The fear is genuine: the man who killed ten thousands of Philistines is now at the mercy of a Philistine king. The vulnerability of the anointed in enemy territory — genuinely endangered — mirrors Christ's genuine vulnerability in the hands of Roman power. The incarnation's condescension involves real vulnerability: Christ was “made perfect through suffering” (Hebrews 2:10) and “learned obedience through what he suffered” (Hebrews 5:8). The anointed's fear is not weakness but the cost of the complete identification with human vulnerability that the redemptive mission requires.
13: David feigns madness — acting insane, making marks on the gateposts, letting saliva run down his beard. The wise man playing the fool to escape from those who would destroy him is the pattern of the incarnation's kenosis: “though he was in the form of God, he did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant” (Philippians 2:6-7). Christ concealing his divine glory in the “weakness” of flesh confounds the powers: “None of the rulers of this age understood this, for if they had, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory” (1 Corinthians 2:8). The hidden wisdom looks like folly.
14: Achish dismisses David: “This man is obviously mad! Why did you bring him to me?” The strategy works — the anointed is dismissed as harmless by the hostile king who does not perceive the truth beneath the appearance. “The message of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God” (1 Corinthians 1:18). The world dismisses the anointed as mad or irrelevant — the carpenter from Galilee, the one who was crucified — and in doing so releases him to accomplish the salvation that the world's hostility cannot prevent.
15: Achish: “Do I need madmen, that you've brought this one to carry on in front of me? Is this man supposed to come into my house?” The hostile king's rejection of the one he perceives as a madman is the world's rejection of the gospel's apparent foolishness. “God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise” (1 Corinthians 1:27). The anointed dismissed as a madman and expelled from the enemy's court is protected by the enemy's own contempt — Achish's dismissal saves David's life. God's “foolish” strategy of the cross is vindicated precisely by the world's contempt for it; the rejected one becomes the cornerstone.
